text:
Powered byOutside

(Photo: Gruber Images / Velo)

Will theParis Olympic Gamesbe treated to aMathieu van der Poelmasterclass on Saturday? Or will Belgium’s time trial “bigs”Remco EvenepoelandWout van Aertput even more froth into the beers of their home fans?



Who knows.

Anything can happen in Olympic racing.

Small teams, no radios, and the attrition of a six-plus-hour race means luck can be as decisive as legs in the race for a four-year title.

The favorites for Paris’ men’s road race are clear, nonetheless.

The 272km course is littered with kicker climbs that accumulate to 2,800m ascent. It’s a “death by a thousand cuts” that could come down to a reduced sprint, a break over the final climb, or even something more ambitious than that.

Classics tough guys with a fast finish will rule the roads Saturday.

Richard Carapaz isn’t there to defend his title after he was controversialy benched by the Ecuadorian team. Tadej Pogačar stirred similar shock when he withdrew his name from the Slovenian selection.

There will be even more surprises playing out on the road Saturday.

Here’s our ranking of the favorites for a wildly unpredictable men’s Olympic road race:

He blazed through the spring but fizzled through the Tour de France. Will Mathieu van der Poel find his [Olympic] flame once more this weekend?

Paris’ hill-packed, monument-distanceparcoursis perfect for the dazzling Dutchman. He could win it from a reduced group, go solo over the final Montmartre kicker, or knowing MVDP, light the burners even earlier.

Ominously for any aspiring podium finisher, Van der Poel claims his legs came good through the third week of an otherwise off-key Tour.

More ominously still, Van der Poel pulled the plug on his MTB dreams to be at max power for the Olympic road race in what is a high-stakes ride for the Dutch star.

If the 29-year-old brings home gold Saturday, he’ll have pretty much completed one-day racing and assured his place in cycling’s history books.

If Van der Poel keeps a cool head and has refound the form that saw him decimate the spring, the gold medal is his to lose.

Van der Poel’s rivals will mark him for every one of Saturday’s 272 kilometers — even when he stops for a pee.

Belgium’sdouble-trouble time trial toppersroar into the road race Saturday with the wind at their backs.

A spectacular 1-3 last weekend saw Remco Evenepoel’s multi-year Olympic project come good in a result that reduces Saturday’s road race to a cheeky bonus.

Third on the podium on the rain-sloshed race against the clock gave Wout van Aert the confirmation he so craved after all his early goals were decimated in a devastating crash at Dwars door Vlaanderen.

Don’t be surprised to see one of Belgium’s ballers on the steps in Paris again Saturday.

On paper, Evenepoel and Van Aert is the perfect pairing.

Evenepoel for the long-ranger over the final Côte de la Butte Montmartre, and Van Aert for a fast finish, what more could Team Belgium want?

Even teammates Jasper Stuyven and Tiesj Benoot could be there with them for the bullishly strong Belgian bloc in a decisive final split.

It’s not always so simple for Evenepoel and Van Aert however.

The temperamental twosome left Belgian fans shedding tears into their beers and made headlines for months when they brought tactical disharmony to the 2021 Leuven worlds.

It will be tough for even a perfect MVDP to defend an Evenepoel-Van Aert double-punch Saturday.

Team Belgium just needs to figure out – and stick to – a fool-proof plan.

Julian Alaphilippeknows how to show up on the world’s biggest stages, and downtown Paris will be the ultimate amphitheater Saturday for the peloton’s favorite showman.

The French star has been eyeing the Olympic road race since Paris was handed hosting rights. He sculpted his 2024 training program around his home Games and skipped the Tour de France in July in service of a glorious golden August.

All signs suggest the plan is working.

For the first time in years, the Alaphilippe of double world title pedigree might be “back.”

Hisagainst-all-odds breakaway victorythis May at the Giro d’Italia was one for the highlights-reel and he’ll parade into Paris stoked by the memory of a timely stage-win last week at the Czech Tour.

Alapahilippe has got both the motivation and the multi-scenario skillset. The fervent French crowds will put further power into Ala-panache’s Paris dream.

But will bypassingLe Tourleave him short on the depth of rivals like Van der Poel and Evenepoel for Saturday’s six hour sufferfest?

Either way, expect fireworks in what could be the 32-year-old’s last chance at Olympic glory.

Biniam Girmaybecame the first black African to win a stage and the green jersey of the Tour de France, and he could be poised to write anotherline in the record booksSaturday.

The Eritrean sensation blasts into Paris hot off the back of a blockbuster Tour de France and buoyed by the hype of being one of the few cyclist flagbearers at the Games’ opening ceremony.

Girmay packs the versatility and classics chops to hang with “bigs” like MVDP and WVA in either a small sprint or a longer-range move.

But what “Bini” doesn’t have a single teammate to help him toward the magic sixth hour of racing.

Yet a lack of teammates might not be too much of a problem for Eritrea’s national hero. The 24-year-old is liberated by the freedom of having little to lose after his career-defining Tour de France.

If Girmay maintained the form that carried him through his three-week Tour de France romp, he should be able to surf the wheels into the final 50km with ease.

From there, history awaits.

Mads Pedersendoesn’t pack the wild road racingpalmarèsof Mathieu van der Poel and Wout van Aert. But let’s face it, where the two classics “Bigs” go, Mads is bumping bars right along side them.

In most circumstances, Denmark’s one-day bullozer would be elbowing right up alongside WVA and MVDP in the rankings for Saturday’s race.

He’s got the kick to survive the Parisien climbs, and the speed to usurp even Van Aert and Girmay in a small bunch sprint.

But how well has Pedersen bounced out of the Tour de France?

The 28-year-old’s race ended on the tarmac in a high-speed crash on stage 5 and he left two days later with the mission to heal up and zone-in on his years-long gold medal ambition.

The lack of recent racing and the potential hangover of his injuries means there will be a question mark over Pedersen when he clicks into his cleats in Paris.

The likes of Van der Poel and Van Aert will be piling all the pressure onto Pedersen on Saturday for fear of his fast finish.

The strong and savvy Danish crew of Mattias Skjelmose, Mikkel Bjerg, and Michael Mørkøv will need to dig deep to deliver Pedersen into the final if he left some watts on the pavement atLe Tour.

If they pull it off, there will be little stopping the burly speedster when he gets a sniff of the finish line.

Matteo Jorgenson and Brandon McNulty (USA):JorgensonandMcNultyline up alongside teammate Magnus Sheffield with a mission to improve on McNulty’s underdog ride to sixth in Tokyo. Jorgenson is hot out of a breakout Tour de France and McNulty is stinging fromhis close call in the TTlast weekend. Team USA’s motivation – and their chances – will be high.

Derek Gee (Canada):What can’t climber-rouleur–puncheurGee do? The Canuck is relishing the best form of his career and he could reap the rewards in Paris. He’ll be pinning his hopes on a small group wrestle into the final rather than a reduced sprint.

Tom Pidcock (Great Britain):Pidcock lost his GC legs at the Tour de France but was on sensational stage-hunting form before he exited the race early with COVID. But heck, even a brush with the ‘rona didn’t hold Pidcock back from a stunningOlympic MTB title defense Mondayon the Elancourt circuit. Coult the Brit bag a third gold medal Saturday? It’s a long shot, but not out of the question either.

Ben Healy (Ireland):Healy’s got the nerve and the verve – just look at his blitz offense on the Tour de France. But the course isn’t ideal for the Irish attacker. Could Healy’s wildcard give him a chance at an early move? He’ll be watched closely.

Final shout-outs to: Alberto Bettiol (Italy), Oier Lazkano (Spain), Jhonatan Narváez (Ecuador), Matej Mohorič (Slovenia)
title: Ranking The Favorites for Men's Olympic Road Race Gold in Paris
author: Jim Cotton
description: Can Van Aert usurp Van der Poel? Will Alaphilippe deliver panache to Paris? We rate the favorites and outsiders for the Olympic road race.
